coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon nanoparticles in a non-thermal plasma process
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cheng
Li, Dongning
Lu, ZhongShan
Song, Ming
Xia, Weidong
coredata.dc:description: A non-thermal plasma source based on magnetically stabilized gliding arc discharge (MSGAD) was used to prepare carbon nanoparticles via methane decomposition. Spherical carbon nanoparticles (SCNs), few-layer graphene nanoflakes (GNFs), and nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles were obtained. The results showed that the product microstructure was influenced by the buffer gas. In pure methane and argon, the products were a mixture of SCNs and GNFs. In helium and hydrogen, all products were highly crystalline GNFs with low defects, few layers, large BET surface areas, and excellent thermal stability. Under a nitrogen atmosphere, nitrogen-doped nanoparticles were formed, and the products were a mixture of GNFs, disordered graphitic layers, and tiny spots similar to carbon dots. The formation of GNFs was possibly related to the high input power and abundant hydrogen atoms, while the complex product morphology obtained under a nitrogen atmosphere was likely caused by the incorporation of nitrogen atoms.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A non-thermal plasma source based on magnetically stabilized gliding arc discharge (MSGAD) was used to prepare carbon nanoparticles via methane decomposition. Spherical carbon nanoparticles (SCNs), few-layer graphene nanoflakes (GNFs), and nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles were obtained. The results showed that the product microstructure was influenced by the buffer gas. In pure methane and argon, the products were a mixture of SCNs and GNFs. In helium and hydrogen, all products were highly crystalline GNFs with low defects, few layers, large BET surface areas, and excellent thermal stability. Under a nitrogen atmosphere, nitrogen-doped nanoparticles were formed, and the products were a mixture of GNFs, disordered graphitic layers, and tiny spots similar to carbon dots. The formation of GNFs was possibly related to the high input power and abundant hydrogen atoms, while the complex product morphology obtained under a nitrogen atmosphere was likely caused by the incorporation of nitrogen atoms.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanoparticles including fullerenes/carbon dots (0D), carbon nanotubes (1D), graphene (2D), and carbon black (3D) have been used in nanotechnology applications due to their unique electronic, optical, thermal, chemical, and mechanical properties (Gonzalez-Aguilar et al., 2007). Despite their advancements, the industrial-scale production of carbon nanoparticles has been limited because they are environmental pollutants (Zahid et al., 2018). For example, carbon black and fullerenes are currently produced on an industrial scale in combustion reactors, which produces copious amounts of greenhouse gases (Gutsch et al., 2002; Murayama et al., 2005). Similarly, the oxidation-reductionmethod can be used for the large-scale fabrication of graphene, but it produces considerable quantities of waste liquid (Zhu et al., 2010). Gas-phase plasma processes provide a reasonable alternative to these carbon nanoparticle synthesis routes due to their numerous advantages, such as environmental friendliness, high value-addedproducts, and adjustable operating conditions (Wang et al., 2019a, 2020b, 2020c; Gonzalez-Aguilar et al., 2007; Fabry et al., 2001; Shavelkina et al., 2018). Plasmas can generally be divided into thermal and non-thermal plasmas (Fridman and Kennedy, 2004). Thermal plasmas are typically hot (gas temperature > 4000 K), and they are regarded as high-energy consumers because a large part of the input electrical power is spent on non-valuable heat. Non-thermal plasmas have lower gas temperature (<3000 K) and higher electron energy (>1 eV), and have attracted much attention in carbon nanoparticle synthesis due to their high chemical activity/selectivity and high energy conversion efficiency.

Several studies on carbon nanoparticle synthesis in non-thermal plasmas have been carried out in the pasttwodecades. For instance, Liu et al. (Zhang et al., 2000) obtained carbon black and carbon nanotubes in non-thermal plasmas via acetylene decomposition, and showed that the addition of H2 was a key factor that affected product appearance. Hong et al. (Zhao et al., 2011; Yuan et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2016) developed a synthetic route to obtain of carbon black in non-thermal plasmas, and their results indicated that reaction temperature and hydrocarbon concentration had significant effects on the carbon black morphology. Zielinski et al. (Zielinski and Kijenski, 2005) first prepared graphene nanoflakes (GNFs) with few layers via a non-thermal plasma, which enabled the non-thermal plasma process more appealing because of the occurrence of high value-added GNFs. Moreno-Couranjou et al. (Moreno-Couranjou et al., 2009; Gonzalez-Aguilar et al., 2007) investigated the product microstructure in a non-thermal plasma process, and suggested that high reaction temperature promoted the transformation of carbon nanoparticles from carbon black to GNFs. Similar research was also carried out by Zhu et al. (Wu et al., 2014, 2017, 2019) and Zhong et al. (Zhong and Hong, 2020). Recently, non-thermal plasma processes based on microwave discharge have been developed to prepare GNFs (Tatarova et al., 2013, 2014; Dato and Frenklach, 2010; Dato, 2019; Dato et al., 2009; Bundaleska et al., 2018; Melero et al., 2017; Rincón et al., 2015); and relevant research has reported the effects of input power, reaction temperature, feedstock type, and reactor design on the formation of GNFs. In reviewing these studies, we found that process parameters such as plasma source, input power, reaction temperature, and feedstock type, are essential to controllably prepare carbon nanoparticles. To our knowledge, little research has systematically investigated the influence of buffer gases on carbon nanoparticles synthesis in non-thermal plasmas. However, some research has shown that the buffer gas is an important factor that affects the product microstructure in other chemical synthesis methods such as chemical vapor deposition (Dacheng et al., 2009; Ivan et al., 2011; Lin et al., 2018) and arc methods (Li et al., 2010, 2017, 2014; Shen et al., 2012; Qin et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2019; Sedelnikova et al., 2019).

Our group has recently developed a non-thermal plasma source based on magnetically stabilized gliding arc discharge (MSGAD) (Wang et al., 2018). This non-thermal plasma may have advantages in carbon nanoparticle synthesis due to its good stability, high non-equilibrium level and simple equipment. In this paper, we attempted the controllable synthesis of carbon nanoparticles using MSGAD. Carbon nanoparticles were prepared under different buffer gases (pure CH4, Ar, He, H2, and N2) via methane decomposition. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), Raman spectroscopy, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy were used to analyze the product microstructures. Based on the product characterization, the mechanism by which the buffer gases affected the carbon nanoparticle formation is discussed.

A basic illustration of the experimental apparatus is exhibited in Fig. 1 , which is primarily comprised of a plasma generator, collectionchamber, gas supply system, DC power system and data acquisition system. The plasma generator is mainly made up of a pair of concentric stainless-steel electrodes. The inner electrode is the cathode with a diameter of 8 mm, and the outer electrode is the anode with an inner diameter of 24 mm. The anode is surrounded by annularmagnets providing an axial magnetic field of 0.2 T. The arc plasma rotates around the cathode at several hundred revolutions per second induced by the magnetic field to form the MSGAD (Wang et al., 2018). The feedstock gas and buffer gas were injected from the cathode bottom to the plasma generator. Fig. 1 shows typical images of MSGAD under different exposuretime. The MSGAD presented a disk-shaped plasma under a long exposuretime due to its high rotational speed. As suggested in Ref (Wang et al., 2018), the electron energy was about 1 eV and the heavy particle temperature was in the range of 1500–3000 K in the plasma region. A stainless-steel collection chamber was connected to the anode, and the collection chamber contained an exhaust exit for gas emissions. A modulated DC power (digital control-constant current power, 0–500 mA, 0–7 kV) was used to generate the plasma, and a data acquisition card (RBH8275, 100 kHz) was used to capture the discharge current and voltage signals.

In the experimental procedure, the buffer gas was first introduced into the plasma generator for plasma generation. Then, the feedstock gas was continuously injected into the plasma region. The feedstock gas was rapidly cracked induced by the plasma to form carbon nanoparticles and other gaseous products. It should be noted especially that the carbon nanoparticles were produced in the gas phase. They flowed along the gas, and then deposited on the inner wall of collectionchamber by their self-gravity or adhesion force. The gaseous products and few carbon nanoparticles were emitted into the atmosphere through the exhaust exit, so the pressure during synthesis was approximately 1 atm.

The input conditions used for each test are shown in Table 1 . The feedstock gas was methane, and its flow rate was 0.2 slm. The buffer gases included argon, helium, hydrogen, and nitrogen, at flow rates of 3 slm. The purity of all gases was more than 99.99%. The discharge current was 0.3 A, and the voltage was changed according to the buffer gases; thus, the input power ranged from 120 to 260 W. About 0.1–0.4 g solid products were achieved in the collection chamber after a 30 min experiment, so the synthesis rate of solid productswas in the range of 3–14 mg min−1 and their yield was in the range of 3–13%.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2011, JEOL), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7401F, JEOL) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension Icon, Bruker) were used to characterize the product morphologies. A Raman spectrometer (LabRam HR Evolution, Horiba Scientific) was used to understand the crystalline structure of the products. Raman spectrum measurements were recorded at a laser excitation wavelength of 514 nm in the spectral range of 500–3000 cm−1. The specific surface area of the products was calculated by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method using a surface area analyzer (Tristar II 3020 M, Micromeritics). The product composition was analyzed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, STA 449F3, Netzsch) with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 up to 900 °C under air. Elemental analysis of the products was performed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos) using monochromatic Al irradiation in the range of 200–700 eV. A Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet IR 200, Thermo Scientific) was used to characterize the functional groups of samples.

Two types of carbon nanoparticles were observed in the TEM images of the products obtained in pure CH4 (Fig. 2 ). One type was sphericalcarbon nanoparticles (SCNs) with a diameter range of 10–30 nm. These SCNs are aggregated and fused to form a branched morphology, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The HRTEM image in Fig. 2(c) shows that the SCNs possess several nuclei with typical concentric structures and many small, distorted graphitic layers enveloping the nuclei. The selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 2(b) displays faint 002, 10 and 11 reflection rings, suggesting that the SCNs had a typical turbostratic structure, which confirms the low level of organization of the graphitic layers. Thus, the SCNs were similar to traditional amorphous carbon black (Fabry et al., 2001; Wang and Hong, 2017). The other product was graphene nanoflakes (GNFs). Fig. 2(a) and (d) show the that GNFs appeared irregularly curled flakes that overlapped and coalescedwith each other, which were referred to “crumpled paper sheet”-like carbon nanoparticles (Moreno-Couranjou et al., 2009). Each nanoflake has a size (lengthandwidth) range of 50–200 nm and consists of 1–20 graphitic layers. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2(e) indicates that the number of layers was 7 and 12, and the lattice spacing of the graphitic layers was about 0.36 nm, which was slightly larger than that of graphite (~0.335 nm). The inset SAED pattern in Fig. 2(d) shows a central bright spot surrounded by concentric rings with spot pattern, confirming that the GNFs were polycrystalline. Some GNFs were significantly aggregated, exhibiting a fuzzy and dense state, as shown in Fig. 2(f). This possibly occurred due to the absence of a buffer gas, resulting in an excessive concentration of GNFs in the plasma region, which collided to form a highly aggregated structure.

Fig. 3 (a–f) display the TEM images of carbon nanoparticles obtained under different buffer gases. Under an Ar atmosphere, SCNs and GNFs coexisted in the products, but highly aggregated GNFs were not observed, which indicates that the addition of Ar can effectively decrease aggregation. When the buffer gas was He, all carbon nanoparticles became GNFs, as shown in Fig. 3(c). These GNFs were irregularly curled, and the size range of the nanoflakes was 50–200 nm. As indicated by the HRTEM image in Fig. 3(d), each nanoflake typically had fewer than 10 graphitic layers with lattice spacing of about 0.36 nm. When the buffer gas was changed to H2, GNFs were the sole product, with a size range of 50–200 nm. Compared with the GNFs obtained in He, the GNFs in H2 appeared to be more transparent, revealing fewer graphitic layers. Moreover, the GNFs obtained in H2 were straighter because the nanoflakes were significantly less curled, as exhibited in Fig. 3(e) and 3(f). The lattice spacing of the graphitic layers decreased to around 0.35 nm, approaching that ofgraphite and indicating a high degree of crystallinity. Fig. 3(g) shows a typical AFM image of the GNFs synthesized in H2, in which two nanoflakes (labeled as 1 and 2) are visible. To better estimate the thickness of the nanoflakes, the height distribution along the contour is also given in Fig. 3(g). The height of nanoflake “1″ is around 2.5 nm, indicating about 7 layers, consistent with the TEM images.

Raman spectroscopy is useful for probing the structural characteristics of graphitic materials. Fig. 4 shows representative Raman spectra of carbon nanoparticles prepared under different buffer gases. Three intense peaks, including the D band (1350 cm−1), G band (1580 cm−1), and 2D band (2680 cm−1), were observed in each spectrum. The D band is called the disorder band, and it commonly appears due to the presence of defects, and the 2D-band is an overtone of the D band. The G band reflects in-plane sp2 carbon vibrations. The relative intensity of the D band to the G band (ID/IG), the peak position and full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the G band are commonly used to quantify the defects and crystallinity of graphene samples (Nemanich and Solin, 1979; Sun et al., 2016). The peak position and FWHM of the 2D band, and the relative intensity of the 2D band to the G band (I2D/IG) are typically used to determine the number of layers in graphene sheets (Casiraghi et al., 2007; Ferrari, 2007). Table 2 summarizes the peak positions and FWHM of the G band, ID/IG values, and I2D/IG values.

When the buffer gas changed, both ID/IG and FWHM values decreased, and the G band shifted slightly to a lower wavenumber, indicating that the carbon nanoparticles have fewer defects and a higher crystallinity. For the products obtained in pure CH4 and Ar, the high defects and low crystallinity were attributed to the existence of SCNs, which have a high degree of defects and disorder. The GNFs formed in H2 have lower ID/IG and FWHM values than those obtained in He, primarily due to the formation of straighter nanoflakes in H2. Moreover, the FWHM of the G band in He and H2 was about 30 cm−1, which is higher than single-layer graphene (14 cm−1) or graphite (12 cm−1) (Ferrari et al., 2003). The relatively high FWHM showed that some defects and disorder were still present in the GNFs (Ferrari and Basko, 2013). According to the TEM images, the GNFs obtained in our experiment were small, which easily resulted in the formation of many wrinkles and edges that were always seen as defects (Pimenta et al., 2007), thus some defects are inevitable. In He and H2, the FWHM of the 2D band was about 60 cm−1, confirming that the GNFs consisted of few graphitic layers. The I2D/IG value of the GNFs in H2 was 0.834, higher than that of He (0.662), which indicates that the GNFs formed in H2 have fewer layers, in accordance with the TEM images in Fig. 3.

Fig. 5 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the products prepared in different buffer gases. Steep adsorption occurred in the high relative pressure region (>0.9P/P 0), along with a typical hysteresis loop in the desorption region. According to the IUPAC classification, these products exhibit a type IV adsorption isotherm, which indicates the formation of mesopores in the products (Shen et al., 2012; Ma et al., 2009). The specific surface areas calculated by the BET method (Table 3 ) were significantly different among these products. The average pore size ranged from 12 to 30 nm, confirming the existence of mesopores in the products. The products obtained in pure CH4 displayed the lowest BET surface area, possibly due to the presence of SCNs and highly aggregated GNFs. The SCNs possessed lower specific surface areas than the GNFs, and the highly aggregated GNFs partially overlapped and coalesced, which also resulted in the loss of accessible surface area (Ma et al., 2009). The BET surface area of the GNFs obtained in H2 was higher than those obtained in He. This difference was mainly caused by the fewer layers of the GNFs obtained in H2 since the specific surface area of graphene usually increases as the number of layers decreases.

The above results show that the addition of He and H2 contributes to the formation of highly crystalline GNFs with large specific surface areas. SEM, XPS, FT-IR, and TGA were used to further characterize the GNFs. The low-magnification SEM images of the products obtained in He and H2 (Fig. 6 ) show that the products were all composed of many nanoflakes that piled together to form aggregates witha flocculent morphology. No apparent SCNs were visible in the images, also confirming the generation of high-purity GNFs.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to further characterize the functional groups present on the surface of the products obtained in He and H2. As shown in Fig. 8 , the FT-IR spectra exhibited representative peaks near 1400, 1630, and 3440 cm−1. The absorption band at 1400 cm−1 was attributed to a tertiary COH stretch.The absorption near 1630 cm−1 corresponded to the OH bending and aromatic CC stretch. The strong and broad absorption at 3440 cm−1 was due to the OH stretch (Nethravathi and Rajamathi, 2008). The FT-IR spectra confirm the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the samples, which is consistent with the XPS spectra in Fig. 7.

Fig. 9 exhibits the TGA curves and differential thermogravimetric (DTG) curves, which allow weight loss temperatures of the samples during combustion to be estimated. The TGA curves under He and H2 show that the products began tolose weight at about 600 °C, and the residual mass approached 0% when the temperature exceeded 850 °C. Moreover, only a single peak near 800 °C was observed in the DTG curves. The products likely had a single form or phase of carbon based on the oxidative reactivity indicated by the peak temperature and rate. The absence of a second DTG peak at lower temperatures (less than600 °C) demonstrates that the products are free of amorphous carbon (Ganguly et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2011). Thus, the TGA results also confirm the high purity of the GNFs obtained our experiment. In H2, the DTG peak was located at about 831 °C, a little higher than that in He (796 °C). This phenomenon indicates that the GNFs obtained in H2 were more crystalline/graphitized, and consequently have a lower reactivity. Using the microwave plasma and arc method, the DTG peak value of GNFs commonly ranges from 500 to 600 °C (Melero et al., 2017; Singh et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2015); which is lower than the DTG peak value in our experiment (800 °C). This indicates that the GNFs synthesized by the procedure herein have excellent thermal stability.

A simple comparison of GNFs obtained in recent research using atmospheric plasmas is exhibited in Table 5 . The experimental data in this work correspond to samples obtained in H2-CH4. Table 5 shows that the synthesized GNFs vary significantly among these methods, primarily in terms of their size ranges, defects/crystallinity (ID/IG value) and specific surface areas. The comparison shows that the GNFs in this work have a high crystallinity and a large specific surface area. Remarkably,the energy cost of the method in the current work could be as low as 0.4 kWh/g, which is much lower than microwave and radio-frequency plasma processes, and close to the arc thermal plasma process recently reported by our group (Wang et al., 2019, 2020). In addition, the non-thermal plasma requires a lower reaction temperature (~2000 K) than thermal plasma processes (>3500 K) (Wang et al., 2018; Pristavita et al., 2011; Meunier et al., 2014), indicating that the non-thermal plasma process has milder reaction conditions, which is conducive to a scale-up mode. Thus, this non-thermal plasma process can compete with other plasma processes used to synthesis GNFs.

When N2 was used as the buffer gas, the product morphology varied greatly, as shown in Fig. 10 . The SEM image indicates that the products obtained in N2 were comprised of smaller particles than those obtained in He and H2. The TEM images show that GNFs and fuzzy nanoparticles coexisted in the products, but the fuzzy nanoparticles were the main product. The GNFs have a smaller size, typically less than 100 nm. The HRTEM images in Fig. 10(c) and (d) indicate that the fuzzy nanoparticles were composed of graphitic layers and nearly spherical tiny spots. These graphitic layers were small and distorted, and exhibited a disordered structure. The SAED pattern in Fig. 10(c) shows faint 002, 10, and 11 reflection rings, also suggesting the low level of organization of the graphitic layers. Tiny spots were distributed among the graphitic layers, each spot with a diameter range of 3–10 nm. Fig. 10(d) shows that the spot has a lattice spacing of about 0.21 nm, which is similar to the (100) facet of graphite (Lu et al., 2009). Considering the size and lattice spacing, the tiny spots are likely carbon dots (Zu et al., 2017; Wang and Qiu, 2016; Zuo et al., 2016). To the authors' knowledge, the unique nitrogen-doped nanoparticles are the first time to be found in the non-thermal plasma process.

Fig. 11 displays the Raman spectrum of the products obtained in N2. Three bands —the D band, G band, and 2D band—were observed in the spectrum, but their relative intensities varied significantly compared with those in Fig. 4. In particular, the ID/IG value was 0.887, which is much higher than the the ID/IG values in He and H2, which indicates more defects in the products obtained in N2. Based on the TEM images in Fig. 10, the presence of abundant disordered graphitic layers may be the main reason for the defects. The G band in the N2 product spectrum displayed a small blue-shift with respect to those obtained in other buffer gases. This phenomenon likely occurred due to the incorporation of N into the graphene lattices, resulting in compressive/tensile strain in the C-C bonds (Shi et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2019). Besides, the BET surface area of the products obtained in N2 was only 69.17 m2/g, which is lower than those of other buffer gases.

The XPS spectrum in Fig. 12 (a) shows that when the buffer gas was N2, a small N 1s peak at 399.9 eV was also present in addition to the C 1s and O 1s peaks. The mass fractions of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen were 96.28%, 2.2%, and 1.52%, respectively. The XPS spectrum confirms the formation of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles, in agreement with the Raman spectra. Fig. 12(b)–(d) show the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra. The C 1s spectrum contained three peaks at 284.7, 285.8, and 288.6 eV, which were assigned to the CC/CC, CN, and CO functional groups (Tatarova et al., 2014; Shi et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2014), respectively. The N 1s spectrum contained two peaks at 399.2 and 401.3 eV, which belonged to the NH and CN groups (Toloza et al., 2017), respectively. The O 1s spectrum contained two peaks at 532.3 and 533.8 eV, which were assigned to the CO and COH/COC groups (Liu et al., 2012), respectively. The above data suggest that the products obtained in N2 were nitrogen-doped nanoparticles that contained hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups.

The formation of carbon nanoparticles is very complex in non-thermal plasmas because the plasma is a multi-component system that contains electrons, atoms, ions, and molecules. Based on previous research (Fabry et al., 2001; Moreno-Couranjou et al., 2009; Dato and Frenklach, 2010; Bundaleska et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2019); the formation of carbon nanoparticles in non-thermal plasmas involves three stages (Fabry et al., 2001): (i) the decomposition of the feedstock into smaller reactive fragments (e.g., H, C, CH, C2…); (ii) the formation of C-X precursors (X: carbon atom or heteroatom), which represents the transformation from a molecular system to a particulate system; (iii) surface growth and agglomeration to form carbon nanoparticles. As indicated by the TEM images, the SCNs have concentric structures with small and distorted graphitic layers, which usually occurs when they form at low temperatures (Fabry et al., 2001; Moreno-Couranjou et al., 2009). In contrast, the lamellar appearance of the GNFs reveals an anisotropic formation process, which typically occurs at high temperatures (Fabry et al., 2001; Moreno-Couranjou et al., 2009; Whitesides and Frenklach, 2009). That is, the coexistence of SCNs and GNFs in pure CH4 is possibly due to the uneven temperature distribution in the plasma region. In principle, Ar and He cannot bond with carbon, but they can affect the plasma parameters such as discharge voltage (input power/temperature) and carbon concentration. When the buffer gas was Ar, the GNF aggregation was decreased because Ar can act as a diluter to decrease the carbon concentration. Some SCNs were still present in the products because of the lower input power (126 W). When the buffer gas changed to He, the input power increased to 195 W due to the high ionization energy of He, which may be an important factor that promoted GNF formation.

The addition of H2 and N2 also changed the required input power (201 W and 255 W). More importantly, the reactive gases can react and bond with carbon during the carbon nanoparticle formation and, thus, affect the final product microstructure. Fig. 13 shows the possible formation pathways for carbon nanoparticles in H2 and N2 atmospheres. It has been claimed that hydrogen can terminate the dangling carbon bonds by forming C-H bonds, thereby maintaining actively growing planar graphene sheets and reducing edge curl (Qin et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 1995). When insufficient hydrogen is present, the dangling carbon bonds cannot be effectively terminated. According to the lowest energy principle (Cotton et al., 1999), SCNs with concentric structures are easily created by the rolling and enclosing of graphitic layers. When the buffer gas was H2, the decomposition of H2 maintained a high concentration of hydrogen in the reaction system, which helped prevent the rolling and enclosing of graphitic layers into SCNs. Hydrogen also etched amorphous carbon (Xie et al., 2009). Consequently, the resultant products formed in an H2 atmosphere tended to be highly crystalline, high-purity GNFs. In arc methods, Li et al. (Li et al., 2014) noted that the number of layers of GNFs depended on the cooling rate during synthesis; and a faster cooling rate usually led to thinner GNFs. We think that the GNFs also follows this law in the non-thermal plasma process. It has been reported that H2 has a more efficient cooling capability than He because of its higher thermal conductivity (Wang et al., 1995). Thus, thinner GNFs are expected to be produced in an H2 atmosphere.

In N2, the high-energy plasma can promote the formation of CN bonds (Wu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2014). The XPS spectra in Fig. 12 also confirm that nitrogen participates in the formation of carbon nanoparticles. The presence of C-N bonds can generate pentagons and heptagons in the graphitic layers; which causes the graphitic layers to bend (Mandumpal et al., 2007; Broitman et al., 2001); thus; distorted graphitic layers were dominant in the products obtained in the N2 atmosphere. However, some hydrogen was always present in the reaction system due to methane decomposition, which is why some GNFs were still observed in the products. The emergence of tiny spots is also surprising. According to their appearance and elemental composition, the tiny spots are very similar to nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Wang et al., 2018). The formation mechanism of these tiny spots is unclear, but our results lead us to believe that adding N2 to non-thermal plasma may be an effective one-step method for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles.

In this paper, MSGAD was used to prepare carbon nanoparticles by methane decomposition. The products obtained in different buffer gases (pure CH4, Ar, He, H2, and N2) were characterized, and the experimental results showed that the characteristics of the carbon nanoparticles depended on the buffer gas. In pure CH4 and Ar, the products were a mixture of SCNs and GNFs. In He and H2, high-quality GNFs with few defects, high crystallinity, few layers, large BET surface areas, and good thermal stability were synthesized. The formation of GNFs was possibly related to the high input power and abundant hydrogen atoms. However, three kinds of products—GNFs, disordered graphitic layers, and tiny spots similar to carbon dots—were formed when N2 was used as the buffer gas. Elemental analysis indicated that the product morphology was likely influenced by the incorporation of nitrogen atoms.

This study shows that the GNFs produced using the non-thermal plasma process based on MSGAD were highly crystalline, had good thermal stability, and low energy requirements, making this process competitive with other plasma processes. Moreover, the product composition obtained in N2 indicates that the non-thermal plasma process also has great potential for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles.

Cheng Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Dongning Li: Methodology, Investigation. ZhongShan Lu: Methodology. Ming Song: Data curation, Validation. Weidong Xia: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.
